item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The testing log is good fun, but the description wastes too much of the article space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
By the way I just realized you're the one who wrote that puppy machine! That one is one of my all time favorites.
Edit: oops wrong person

item.title: Thank you very much for the feedback.
item.content:encoded:
Sorry for the late reply. I fixed some things you mentioned, and as for the second-to-last test, I replaced it with something much more tasteful and less cliche.
Again, thank you for the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for bringing that up! I fixed a few things and replaced the cliche stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea behind this. But I do have some concerns regarding it:

  • A minimum of 15,536 people played this game before the Foundation discovered it. And it's clear that the game was online for multiple months (otherwise, there would be no monthly average). It's fair to assume that many thousands of messages were left before the Foundation took the game offline. That's… that's gotta be a lot of random events - all of which reference pop culture in some way - occurring all over the world. And considering how many famous films and TV shows are set in heavily populated areas (NYC, for example)… well, the idea that people wouldn't notice that something was up is a little far-fetched. I'd suggest making it harder for people to leave anomalous messages. Maybe the message only sets off a real-world event if the player gets a particularly high score, or only when they reach the top of an especially challenging building.
  • What's stopping the players from typing a completely original phrase in as their message? Why are they seemingly limited to pop culture references?
  • That second-to-last test, which… I, uh. I think I'm too young to accurately judge the tastefulness of that one.

The concept here is definitely interesting, and I'd love to see it succeed. But I do think it needs some revising in order to be successful. For now, I'm not casting a vote either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reasons for my downvote:

  • The description is way more convoluted than it needs to be.
  • I'm not a scientist, but the "5000% increase in nuclear radiation" sets off serious realism red flags for me. Cursory research indicates that 5000% of the average background radiation in Ibaraki would be about 0.7 millirems, max. You're exposed to 40 millirems at the dentist. Furthermore, a "10-cm-wide area" would be two-dimensional, and even a 10cm2 area would be ridiculously hard to detect within a whole country in the 13 seconds it manifested.
  • The [DATA EXPUNGED] noodle incident is really cliche.
  • The 682 reference is really, really cliche.

Overall, it's a interesting enough idea, but the execution could use more work.

item.title: My first published skip, based off a really weird dream I had the other night.
item.content:encoded:
Hey everybody,

I had a lot of fun writing this one. I know the basic idea is very random, but it was all based off a dream I had, and I couldn't control what happened in my dream!
Anyways, I hope you enjoyed reading my skip as much as I enjoyed writing it. I'm planning on writing some extended test logs soon!
Any feedback you guys have is greatly appreciated.
